WEEK 10

Forecasting with
Time Series Data

MG628 · Data Analysis for Managerial Decision-Making
MBA · MHA · MPA
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▲

Welcome, everyone, to Week 10. This week we are stepping into one of the most powerful skill sets you will carry out of this program — the ability to look at historical data and make confident projections about the future.

Whether you are in the MBA, Healthcare, or Public Administration track, forecasting is not optional. It is how leaders get ahead of problems instead of reacting to them. Let us dive in.

ROADMAP

How Today’s Lecture Builds Step by Step

1

Understand the data

What is a time series? What does each row mean? Why does the Date column matter?

2

See patterns visually

We first plot the data to identify trend, seasonality, cycles, noise, and unusual values.

3

Turn patterns into forecasts

We use moving averages, regression, Holt-Winters, ARIMA, and SARIMA to predict future values.

Beginner promise: Every technical term in this lecture will be translated into plain language before it is used in a formula or code block.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Tell students that the lecture is not just about running Python. The purpose is to understand the logic behind each forecasting step so they can explain results to managers, hospital administrators, or public officials.

MOTIVATION

Why Forecasting Is a Leadership Superpower

🏢

MBA

Predict inventory demand for a holiday surge — or face a costly stockout and lost revenue.

🏛️

MPA

Forecast tax revenue and utility demand so infrastructure is ready before the population outgrows it.

🏥

MHA

Project patient volumes and disease outbreaks for proactive staffing and resource allocation.

Leaders who anticipate outperform those who react.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Let us start with why this matters. Think about what separates a reactive manager from a strategic leader. It is the ability to look ahead.

In business, that might mean making sure your shelves are stocked before the holiday rush — not scrambling after the fact. In public administration, it means funding infrastructure before a growing population overwhelms it. In healthcare, it means staffing your ER before flu season peaks, not after patients are already waiting.

Forecasting is that edge. And this week, you are going to learn how to build it.

FOUNDATIONS

What Is Time Series Data?

A time series is a sequence of data points collected at successive, equally-spaced points in time.

Everyday Examples

  • Monthly retail sales over 5 years
  • Weekly ER admission counts
  • Daily COVID-19 case reports by county
  • Annual municipal tax revenue
  • Hourly electricity consumption

⚙️ Key Requirement

Data must be at regular, equal intervals. Without this, time-based operations like rolling averages break down.

Daily Weekly Monthly Quarterly Annual
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

So what exactly is time series data? Simply put, it is any dataset where we have recorded the same measurement repeatedly over time — at regular, equal intervals. Think monthly sales figures, weekly hospital admissions, or daily case counts.

The key word here is equally spaced. We need consistent intervals to detect patterns. Our course dataset — sales_data.csv — uses monthly sales observations. This is the file you will use for both Activity 1 and Activity 2 this week.

VOCABULARY

Basic Terms Before We Touch Python

TermPlain-English MeaningIn Our Notebook
ObservationOne recorded data point.One month of sales.
VariableThe thing we are measuring.Sales.
Date indexThe time label attached to each observation.Month-end dates from 2000 to 2016.
LagA past value of the same variable.Last month’s sales are lag 1.
ForecastA prediction for a future time period.Next 12 monthly sales values.
A time series is not just a list of numbers. It is a list of numbers where order matters.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Emphasize that time order is the difference between ordinary data analysis and time series analysis. Shuffling rows would destroy the meaning of the data.

NOTEBOOK DATA

The Dataset Behind Today’s Examples

Notebook dataset: 200 monthly sales observations from January 2000 through August 2016.
Mean sales
199.80
Std. deviation
58.61
Range
95.29–299.08
DateSales
2000-01-31102.48
2000-02-29103.42
2000-03-31111.15
2000-04-30118.75
2000-05-31112.38
These values come from the executed notebook output and should anchor the lecture examples in the actual class dataset.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Use this slide to connect the abstract concepts to the exact dataset used in the notebook. Students should see that every plot and calculation in the lecture comes from the same monthly sales series.

PYTHON BASICS

Why We Convert the Date Column

# Load the CSV file from GitHub into a pandas DataFrame.
df = pd.read_csv('sales_data.csv')

# Convert the Date column from ordinary text into a real date.
df['Date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['Date'])

# Sort rows so the oldest month comes first and the newest month comes last.
df = df.sort_values('Date')

# Make Date the index so Python treats the data as time-ordered.
df = df.set_index('Date')

What is a DataFrame?

A table in Python. Think of it like an Excel sheet with rows and columns.

What is an index?

The row label. For time series, the index should usually be the date or time.

NOTEBOOK DATA

What the Notebook Dataset Contains

Dataset Snapshot

Rows200 monthly observations
Date rangeJanuary 2000 through August 2016
Main variableSales
Mean sales199.80
Minimum / Maximum95.29 / 299.08

Why this dataset is useful for learning

  • It has an obvious upward trend.
  • It has repeating seasonal movement.
  • It is clean enough for students to focus on forecasting concepts.
  • It is long enough for train/test evaluation.
Teaching point: A forecasting model learns from the past pattern in the 200 rows, then projects that pattern forward.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

This slide grounds the lecture in the actual notebook. The data is monthly sales data with 200 observations, so students should understand that every calculation is built from those 200 rows.

VISUALIZATION

Step 1: Always Visualize First

"A picture of your data reveals patterns that statistics alone will hide. Before you model — plot."
1

Line Plot

Raw series over time — spot trend, outliers, data gaps

2

Seasonal Subseries

Same month across years — confirm seasonality

3

ACF / Lag Plot

Detect autocorrelation cycles and repeating patterns

4

Decomposition

Separate T, S, C, I visually in one chart

# Comment: Load the data as a dated time series, then draw the first line chart before modeling.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
df = pd.read_csv('sales_data.csv', parse_dates=['Date'], index_col='Date')
df['Sales'].plot(title='Monthly Sales Over Time', figsize=(12,5))
plt.xlabel('Date'); plt.ylabel('Sales'); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
Notebook output: Monthly sales line plot
The actual notebook plot shows sales rising from roughly 100 to nearly 300 over 2000–2016, with repeating seasonal fluctuations.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Before you write a single line of forecasting code, always visualize your data first. This is the most important habit I want you to build. A line plot of your raw series will immediately show you whether there is a trend, whether there are obvious seasonal spikes, and whether there are any outliers or data gaps you need to handle.

I cannot count the number of analysts who jumped straight into building models and missed something obvious that a ten-second chart would have revealed. Plot first. Always.

PYTHON

Setting Up Your Python Environment

# Comment: Import the libraries needed for data handling, plotting, stationarity tests, and forecasting models.
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from statsmodels.tsa.seasonal import seasonal_decompose
from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import adfuller
from statsmodels.tsa.holtwinters import ExponentialSmoothing
from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA

# Load your data — always parse_dates!
df = pd.read_csv('sales_data.csv', parse_dates=['Date'], index_col='Date')
print(df.index.dtype)  # Should show: datetime64[ns]
df.head()
  • pandas — data loading, date indexing, rolling calculations
  • matplotlib / seaborn — plotting and visualization
  • statsmodels — decomposition, ARIMA, Exponential Smoothing
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Here is everything you need to import to get through this week's work. You already know pandas and matplotlib from earlier modules. The new library this week is statsmodels — this is the workhorse for time series in Python. It gives us seasonal decomposition, the Augmented Dickey-Fuller test for stationarity, Exponential Smoothing, and ARIMA.

Notice we are using parse_dates and index_col when loading the CSV — this tells pandas to treat the Date column as a proper datetime index. Without it, your plots will show numbers instead of dates, and rolling calculations will fail. Always verify with df.index.dtype — it should show datetime64[ns].

PYTHON

Plotting Your Time Series — Activity 2, Q1

Activity 2 Q1 asks you to plot Sales over time and describe any visible patterns. The chart is easy — the written description is where the grade is.

What to look for in your chart:

  • Is the series going up, down, or flat over time? That is the trend.
  • Are there regular spikes or dips at the same time each year? That is seasonality.
  • Are there sudden drops or jumps that don't fit the pattern? Those are irregular events.

Example Q1 Description

"The sales series shows a clear upward trend over the observation period, with sales growing from approximately 100 units at the start to over 180 units by the end. There is also a visible seasonal pattern — sales appear to peak mid-year in a regular, repeating cycle. A few isolated spikes may represent irregular events or data anomalies."

🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Take a moment and look at your plot. What do you see? First — is there an overall trend? Second — do you see any repeating dips or peaks that seem to happen regularly? Third — are there any sudden, unexpected jumps or drops?

Write two or three sentences that answer those three questions. Do not just say 'I see a trend.' Say what kind of trend, which direction it is heading, and approximately how large the change is over the observation period.

VISUALIZATION

How to Read the First Time Series Plot

X-axis

The time order of observations. Here it runs from 2000 to 2016.

Y-axis

The value being measured. Here it is monthly sales.

Line pattern

The line rises over time, so sales have a clear upward trend.

Question to ask students: Is the general direction upward, downward, or flat? That answer identifies the trend.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Use this slide to teach students how to read a chart before discussing formulas. The first skill is visual interpretation: direction, recurring peaks, unusual points, and variability.

FOUNDATIONS

The DNA of a Time Series: Four Components

T

Trend

The long-term upward or downward direction in the data.

S

Seasonality

Regular, repeating patterns (e.g., higher ER visits every winter).

C

Cyclical

Long-term oscillations (often economic) without a fixed period.

I

Irregular

Random "noise" or unpredictable black-swan events.

Data = Trend + Seasonality + Cyclical + Irregular  (Additive Model)
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Every time series you will ever encounter is made up of four building blocks — think of them as the DNA of your data. First, the Trend — is the overall direction going up, down, or flat over the long run? Second, Seasonality — are there patterns that repeat at the same time every year? Third, Cyclical movements — longer waves that follow economic or political cycles, without a fixed schedule. And fourth, Irregular variation — the unpredictable noise, the one-off events, the things no model can anticipate.

Our goal this week is to separate these components so we can see what is really driving the data.

COMPONENTS

Trend, Seasonality, Cycle, and Noise Are Different

ComponentPlain MeaningTimingExample
TrendThe long-run direction.Years or many periods.Sales steadily grow over time.
SeasonalityA repeating calendar pattern.Fixed period such as 12 months.December holiday sales spike every year.
CycleA broad rise-and-fall pattern.Not fixed; often several years.Recession and recovery.
Noise / IrregularRandom movement not explained by the model.Unpredictable.A strike, storm, pandemic shock, or data error.
Forecasting improves when we can separate these pieces instead of treating every movement as random.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Students often confuse seasonality and cyclical variation. Stress that seasonality repeats on a fixed calendar schedule, while cycles do not have a fixed timing.

TREND

Understanding Trend

  • The long-term, sustained direction of a series
  • Upward — consistent growth over years
  • Downward — systematic decline over time
  • Flat — stable, no meaningful direction
  • Can be linear or curved (non-linear)

📈 Upward Trend (illustrative)

Each period generally higher than the last = upward trend

🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Trend is the big picture — if you zoom out and look at your data across years, is it drifting upward, downward, or holding steady? That drift is the trend.

Data bounces up and down from month to month — that is noise. A trend is what you see when you step back and squint. The 12-month moving average is the best visual tool to reveal the trend by smoothing out the monthly noise. In Activity 2, you will extract exactly this from your sales_data.csv file.

SEASONALITY

Seasonality: Patterns That Repeat

Key Traits

  • Repeats on a fixed, known period
  • Predictable once identified
  • Can be additive or multiplicative
  • Most actionable pattern for planning

Sector Examples

🛍️ Retail: Sales peak in November–December (holiday season)

🏥 Healthcare: ER admissions surge every winter (flu season)

🏛️ Government: Budget requests and tax revenue spike quarterly

🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Seasonality is the pattern that comes back reliably, like clockwork. Retail sales spike every November and December — that is seasonal. ER visits peak every winter as flu season hits — seasonal. Tax filing deadlines cause workload spikes every April — seasonal.

What makes seasonality powerful is that it is predictable. Once you have identified the seasonal pattern, you can plan for it. You can staff up in advance. You can order inventory early. You can budget for the surge before it arrives. This is where forecasting directly drives operational decisions.

COMPONENTS

Cyclical Patterns & Irregular Variations

🔄 Cyclical Variation

  • Waves lasting 2–10+ years
  • Tied to economic or political cycles
  • No fixed, predictable period
  • Example: GDP contractions, housing cycles

⚡ Irregular / Random

  • Cannot be predicted or modeled
  • 'Black swan' events: pandemic, natural disaster
  • Creates residual after removing T, S, C
  • Example: COVID-19 impact on patient volumes
Goal: Model T + S + C accurately → what remains is the Irregular residual

Illustrative Plot 1: Output / Business Activity Cycle

A cyclical pattern moves in longer waves rather than fixed seasonal intervals. Real events such as the 2008–09 financial crisis, the 2020 COVID‑19 shock, and the 2022–23 rate‑hike slowdown can push the cycle downward or change its shape.

Illustrative Plot 2: Unemployment Response to Downturns

Cyclical downturns often show up as job losses. Notice how unemployment spikes during recessions and shocks, then gradually falls during recovery. That rise-and-fall wave is what we mean by cyclical variation.

Teaching note: these are illustrative plots drawn to explain the concept using real event labels. They are not exact official statistics.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

The third component is cyclical variation — longer waves that follow economic or political cycles. Think of a recession: business slows down, revenues dip, and then they recover. That pattern might unfold over three to seven years. Unlike seasonality, cyclical patterns do not have a fixed schedule.

The fourth component is irregular variation — the true wild cards. The pandemic. An unexpected natural disaster. A one-time regulatory change. These cannot be modeled. What we do instead is model everything else well, and treat what remains as the irregular residual. That residual tells us how much of our data we still cannot explain.

FOUNDATIONS

Stationarity: A Critical Requirement

✅ Stationary Series

  • Constant mean over time
  • Constant variance
  • Required for ARIMA models

❌ Non-Stationary Series

  • Mean drifts upward or downward
  • Variance may grow over time
  • Must be transformed before ARIMA
Fix: Differencing — subtract each observation from the previous one. This removes the trend and makes most business series stationary.

df['diff1'] = df['Sales'].diff()
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Before we can apply advanced forecasting models like ARIMA, we need to talk about stationarity. A stationary time series is one where the statistical properties — the mean and variance — stay constant over time. It does not trend upward or downward, and it does not become more or less volatile as time passes.

Most real-world business data is non-stationary, and that is fine. We just need to transform it first. The most common technique is differencing — we subtract each observation from the previous one, which removes the trend and stabilizes the mean. We cover the formal test for this — the ADF test — on slide 23.

FOUNDATIONS

Visualizing Stationarity and Differencing

1) Stationary

The series fluctuates around a fairly stable center. The average level and spread do not drift much over time.

2) Non-Stationary

The series has a visible trend, changing mean, or changing variance. ARIMA usually should not be applied until this is corrected.

3) After Differencing

First differencing computes Yt − Yt−1. This often removes trend and makes the transformed series much closer to stationary.

What students should notice: the middle plot drifts upward, while the differenced plot returns to oscillating around a more stable mean near zero.
Illustrative teaching plots — used to explain the concept visually.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

This slide makes the concept visual. The stationary series moves up and down, but it stays centered around roughly the same mean and has roughly similar volatility. The non-stationary series clearly trends upward, so its mean is not constant over time. That violates one of the core assumptions for ARIMA.

The third plot shows the result of first differencing. Each point is the current observation minus the previous observation. Once we difference the trending series, the upward drift is mostly removed, and the series now fluctuates around a stable center near zero. That is why differencing is such an important step in ARIMA modeling.

STATIONARITY

Differencing: Making a Series Stationary

First Difference

Y't = Yt − Yt−1
# Comment: First differencing subtracts the previous value; seasonal differencing subtracts the value from 12 months ago.
df['diff1'] = df['Sales'].diff()

Seasonal Difference

Y't = Yt − Yt−12
# Comment: First differencing subtracts the previous value; seasonal differencing subtracts the value from 12 months ago.
df['sdiff'] = df['Sales'].diff(12)

What Differencing Does

  • Converts levels to changes — removes the trend
  • First diff (d=1) → removes linear trend
  • Seasonal diff (D=1) → removes annual seasonality
  • Re-run ADF after each diff — stop when p < 0.05
  • Over-differencing introduces noise — stop early!
Notebook output: first difference plot
Differencing converts sales levels into month-to-month changes, helping remove trend.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Differencing is the most important transformation in time series analysis. Instead of working with the raw sales values, we work with the month-to-month changes. This removes the trend — if sales grow by a constant amount each month, the differences will be roughly constant, and the drift disappears.

The critical rule: only difference as many times as necessary. Over-differencing creates artificial patterns. Run the ADF test after each round and stop as soon as the series is stationary. For most business data, one round of differencing is usually enough.

MANUAL CALCULATION

How Differencing Is Calculated by Hand

First difference = Current sales − Previous month’s sales
MonthSalesManual first difference
Jan 2000102.48Not available — no prior month
Feb 2000103.42103.42 − 102.48 = +0.94
Mar 2000111.15111.15 − 103.42 = +7.73
Apr 2000118.75118.75 − 111.15 = +7.60

Python equivalent

df['Diff_1'] = df['Sales'].diff()
Interpretation: Differencing changes the question from “What is the sales level?” to “How much did sales change from last month?” That often removes trend and supports ARIMA modeling.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

This is the easiest stationarity transformation for students to understand manually. Emphasize that the first row becomes missing because there is no previous observation to subtract.

STATIONARITY

Why Stationarity Matters Before ARIMA

ARIMA assumes the rules are stable

If the average and spread keep changing, the model is trying to learn from a moving target. Differencing helps stabilize the series.

Notebook result

ADF Statistic: -0.5257
p-value: 0.8869

Because p-value is much larger than 0.05, the original sales series is non-stationary.

Decision rule: p-value < 0.05 means stationary; p-value ≥ 0.05 means non-stationary.
In plain language: The test says the original sales data still has a changing pattern, so ARIMA needs differencing before it can work properly.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

This slide connects the abstract stationarity idea to the actual notebook output. Students should understand that the p-value is a decision aid, not just a number to report.

STATIONARITY

Testing for Stationarity: The ADF Test

# Comment: Run the ADF test to decide whether the series is stationary before fitting ARIMA.
from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import adfuller

result = adfuller(df['Sales'])
print(f"ADF Statistic: {{result[0]:.4f}}")
print(f"p-value:       {{result[1]:.4f}}")

if result[1] < 0.05:
    print("STATIONARY — ready for ARIMA.")
else:
    print("NON-STATIONARY — apply differencing and re-test.")

✅ p < 0.05 → Reject H₀ → Series is stationary → proceed to ARIMA

⚠️ p ≥ 0.05 → Fail to reject H₀ → Apply differencing, then re-test

Notebook result: ADF test
ADF statistic
-0.5257
p-value
0.8869
Decision
Non-stationary
Interpretation: Because p = 0.8869 is greater than 0.05, the series is non-stationary. We should difference it before fitting ARIMA.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Before fitting any ARIMA model, always run the Augmented Dickey-Fuller test. The null hypothesis is that the series is non-stationary. If p < 0.05, we reject the null and conclude the series is stationary — proceed to ARIMA. If p ≥ 0.05, the series is non-stationary, and we need to difference it.

Difference the data, run the ADF test again, and repeat until the series is stationary. For most business data with a linear trend, one round of differencing — setting d equal to one — is usually enough.

DECOMPOSITION

Additive vs. Multiplicative Models

FeatureAdditiveMultiplicative
FormulaY = T + S + C + IY = T × S × C × I
When to useSeasonal swings are constant in sizeSeasonal swings grow with the trend
ExampleSales vary by ±$10K every DecemberSales vary by ±10% every December
TransformationNone neededLog-transform to make additive
Rule of thumb: if seasonal peaks grow taller as the trend rises → use Multiplicative.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Once we know that a time series has multiple components, we have to decide how those components combine. The Additive model says: the total value equals the sum of all components. Use this when the seasonal swings are constant in size — sales always go up by about ten thousand dollars in December, regardless of how high the trend is.

The Multiplicative model says the components multiply together. Use this when the seasonal swings grow as the trend grows — sales always spike by ten percent in December, so a bigger trend means a bigger absolute swing. When in doubt, plot your data. If the seasonal peaks and troughs are getting taller as the series grows, you need the multiplicative model.

MODEL CHOICE

How Decomposition Models Work: Additive, Multiplicative & Log-Additive

1) Additive Model

Y = Trend + Seasonal + Residual

Meaning: each component adds a fixed amount to the observed value.

Use when: seasonal ups and downs stay about the same size over time.

Example: December sales are usually about +$20,000 above normal every year.

2) Multiplicative Model

Y = Trend × Seasonal × Residual

Meaning: each component changes the observed value by a percentage or ratio.

Use when: seasonal swings get larger as the overall trend gets larger.

Example: December sales are usually about 20% higher than normal.

3) Log-Additive Shortcut

log(Y) = log(T) + log(S) + log(R)

Meaning: taking logs turns multiplicative patterns into additive ones.

Use when: the data grows fast, has percentage-based seasonality, or variance increases over time.

How it works: the model estimates a smooth trend, estimates the typical seasonal effect for each month, then subtracts or divides those components from the observed data. What remains is the residual — the part the model did not explain.
Visual PatternBest ChoiceManagerial Interpretation
Peaks and dips are about the same height every yearAdditiveSeasonality adds a fixed number of units or dollars
Peaks and dips grow as the series growsMultiplicativeSeasonality changes sales by a percentage
Trend rises and volatility expandsLog-additive / MultiplicativeStabilizes variance and makes growth easier to model
No clear trend or seasonalitySimple smoothing / baselineDo not force complex structure into simple data
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

This slide explains the logic behind the model choice. In an additive model, the seasonal effect is measured in fixed units. If December is always about twenty thousand dollars higher than normal, use additive. In a multiplicative model, the seasonal effect is measured as a ratio or percentage. If December is usually twenty percent higher than normal, and the dollar size of that increase grows as the company grows, use multiplicative.

The log-additive approach is a practical bridge. By taking the log of the series, percentage changes behave like additive changes. This is why analysts often log-transform business data before decomposition or regression when the trend and variance grow together.

DECOMPOSITION

Seasonal Decomposition with Python

# Comment: Split the original Sales series into observed, trend, seasonal, and residual components.
from statsmodels.tsa.seasonal import seasonal_decompose

result = seasonal_decompose(df['Sales'], model='additive', period=12)
result.plot()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Access individual components
trend    = result.trend
seasonal = result.seasonal
residual = result.resid

Panel 1

Observed — your original raw data series

Panel 2

Trend — smooth long-run direction

Panel 3

Seasonal — the repeating annual pattern

Panel 4

Residual — what remains after T + S are removed

Notebook output: seasonal decomposition
The decomposition separates the observed series into trend, seasonal, and residual components.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Python's statsmodels library gives us a one-function shortcut for decomposition. seasonal_decompose takes your series and the model type and automatically separates out the trend, seasonal, and residual components, giving you four stacked charts.

Panel four is the residual — the leftover noise. Ideally, it should look like white noise: random, no patterns. If you see patterns in the residual, it means your model did not fully capture all the structure in the data. That is your signal to try the multiplicative model or to check whether period=12 is correct.

NOTEBOOK PLOT

Reading the Decomposition Plot

Observed

Original sales data.

Trend

Long-run direction after smoothing.

Seasonal

Repeating within-year pattern.

Residual

Leftover movement not explained by trend or seasonality.

🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Use this plot to show that decomposition is like taking the original series apart. Students should be able to explain each panel in plain language.

TREND ANALYSIS

Moving Average: Smoothing the Noise

MA(k) = (Yt + Yt-1 + ... + Yt-k+1) / k
  • k = window size (use 12 for monthly data)
  • Each point = average of the surrounding k values
  • Removes short-term fluctuations to reveal the trend
  • Larger k = smoother line, but loses more data endpoints

🎯 Interpretation Guide

📈 Rising smoothed line → upward trend

➡️ Flat smoothed line → trend leveling off

📉 Falling smoothed line → downward trend

# Comment: Create a 12-month rolling average and plot it against the original Sales series.
df['MA12'] = df['Sales'].rolling(12).mean()

df[['Sales', 'MA12']].plot(figsize=(12,5))
plt.title('Sales with 12-Month Moving Average')
plt.legend(['Original', '12-Month MA'])
plt.show()

Averaging exactly 12 consecutive months covers one full year. Every seasonal peak and trough gets included exactly once — so they cancel out. What remains is the pure underlying trend, free of seasonal distortion.

Notebook output: 12-month moving average
Start MA
111.96

Dec. 2000

End MA
287.75

Aug. 2016

Total change
+157.0%

Notebook trend analysis

🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

The Moving Average is our first tool for isolating the trend. Instead of plotting the raw sales for each month — which includes all that seasonal noise — we replace each data point with the average of the surrounding twelve months. That smooths out all the peaks and valleys that repeat each year, leaving us with just the underlying direction.

Why twelve? Because we have monthly data, and averaging twelve consecutive months covers exactly one full year — so every seasonal peak and trough gets averaged in exactly once, and they cancel each other out. Activity 2 Question 2 asks you to plot both on the same chart and describe what the smoothed line reveals that the raw series hides.

NOTEBOOK PLOT

What the Moving Average Reveals

Original line

Shows every monthly up-and-down movement. It is useful, but noisy.

12-month moving average

Smooths one full year at a time, making the long-run trend easier to see.

From the notebook: the moving average rose from 111.96 to 287.75, an increase of about 157%.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

This slide uses the actual notebook plot and results. Emphasize that smoothing is not forecasting by itself; it helps reveal the trend pattern.

MANUAL CALCULATION

How the 12-Month Moving Average Is Calculated by Hand

The first 12-month moving average is just the average of the first 12 monthly sales values.
MonthSales
Jan 2000102.48
Feb 2000103.42
Mar 2000111.15
Apr 2000118.75
May 2000112.38
Jun 2000113.85
Jul 2000123.41
Aug 2000118.90
Sep 2000111.47
Oct 2000114.71
Nov 2000107.58
Dec 2000105.47

Manual calculation

MA12 for Dec 2000 = (102.48 + 103.42 + 111.15 + ... + 105.47) / 12
= 1,343.57 / 12
= 111.96

Next rolling value

MA12 for Jan 2001 = drop Jan 2000, add Jan 2001
= (1,343.57 - 102.48 + 107.24) / 12
= 1,348.32 / 12 = 112.36
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Walk students through why the first 11 moving-average values are blank. The 12th month is the first time we have a full 12-month window. Then show how the window rolls forward by dropping the oldest value and adding the newest one.

BEGINNER CHECK

Why the First 11 Moving Average Values Are Blank

What students see

In the notebook, the first 11 rows of MA_12 show NaN.

NaN means “Not a Number.” It appears when Python cannot calculate a value yet.

Why it happens

A 12-month moving average needs 12 months of data. In month 1, we only have 1 value. In month 11, we only have 11 values. The first complete 12-month average appears in month 12.

# Create a 12-month moving average.
# window=12 tells Python to average 12 rows at a time.
df['MA_12'] = df['Sales'].rolling(window=12).mean()
First MA value = average of months 1 through 12
TREND ANALYSIS

Linear Regression: Quantifying the Trend

Sales = β₀ + β₁ × Time + ε
  • β₀ = intercept (starting value when time = 0)
  • β₁ = slope (average change per time period)
  • ε = residual error (what the model doesn't explain)
  • If β₁ = 2.5 → sales grow by $2,500 per month on average
  • Positive slope = upward trend; Negative slope = downward trend
# Comment: Create a time counter, fit a straight-line trend, then use the slope to describe average growth.
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
import numpy as np

df['time_idx'] = np.arange(len(df))
X = df[['time_idx']]
y = df['Sales']

model = LinearRegression().fit(X, y)
slope = model.coef_[0]
print(f"Slope: {{slope:.3f}} units/month")

df['trend_line'] = model.predict(X)
df[['Sales', 'trend_line']].plot()
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

The second trend technique is linear regression. Where the moving average shows you the trend visually by smoothing the line, regression gives you a precise number — the slope.

The slope tells you, on average, how much sales change for every one unit increase in time. If the slope is 2.5, that means sales grow by about two thousand five hundred dollars every single month, on average. That is a powerful, concrete statement for a manager to make to a leadership team. Activity 2 Question 3 asks you to report the slope and interpret it in plain English — not just as a bare number.

NOTEBOOK RESULT

Regression Trend Results from the Notebook

Intercept100.1590
Slope1.0014
R²0.9778
Interpretation: Sales increased by about 1.00 unit per month on average across the dataset.
Regression is useful because it turns the visual trend into a single number: the slope.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Explain that the red regression line is a summary of the long-run direction. It does not capture every seasonal up and down, but it gives a clear estimate of average monthly growth.

MANUAL CALCULATION

How the Regression Trend Slope Is Interpreted

Sales = β₀ + β₁ × Time

Manual idea

β₁ = Σ[(x - x̄)(y - ȳ)] / Σ[(x - x̄)²]

The slope is the average change in sales for each one-period increase in time.

Notebook result

Intercept = 100.1590
Slope = 1.0014
R² = 0.9778

Interpretation: sales increased by about 1.00 unit per month on average across the full series.

🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Students do not need to hand-compute the slope for all 200 observations, but they should understand what the formula is doing: it compares deviations in time with deviations in sales and converts that relationship into an average monthly change.

TREND ANALYSIS

Moving Average vs. Regression — Activity 2, Q4

CriterionMoving AverageLinear Regression
OutputSmoothed line (variable shape)Straight line (fixed slope)
Handles non-linearity?Yes — follows curvesNo — assumes straight line
Loses data?Yes — first k-1 points missingNo — full series used
Gives a number?NoYes — slope with p-value
Best for…Visualization & explorationReporting & hypothesis testing
Use both: MA to see the shape of the trend, Regression to quantify and test it. They are complementary, not competing.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Activity 2 Question 4 asks you to compare these two methods. Here is the key insight: they are not competing tools — they are complementary. The moving average is your visual tool. It shows you the shape of the trend. Is it curving? Is it leveling off? Is there a kink somewhere where growth accelerated?

The regression line is your reporting tool. It gives you a single, precise number — the slope — that you can present to a board, a committee, or a grant reviewer. In practice, professionals use both. Use the moving average to explore and communicate the pattern, then use regression to quantify and test it.

LIMITATIONS

Limitations of MA & Regression — Activity 2, Q5

Moving Average

  • Loses k-1 data points at the start of the series
  • Cannot forecast future values directly
  • Does not model seasonality
  • Reacts to trend changes with a lag

Linear Regression

  • Assumes straight-line trend (often wrong in reality)
  • Sensitive to outliers distorting the slope
  • Ignores autocorrelation between observations
  • Cannot capture seasonal patterns alone
These are exploratory tools — great for understanding data, but not the final forecasting step.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Activity 2 Question 5 asks you to discuss limitations — so let me build your answer here. For the moving average: it is inherently backward-looking and cannot project into the future. It loses data at the beginning because the first window is not complete. And it has no awareness of seasonal patterns.

For regression: the assumption of a straight-line trend is often wrong. More critically, regression assumes that observations are independent of each other — which is almost never true for time series. Each month's sales are correlated with the previous month. Violating this assumption means our confidence intervals and hypothesis tests are unreliable. These are exploration and communication tools — they set the stage for the more powerful models we build next.

ASSIGNMENT WALKTHROUGH

Week 10 Assignment: What Students Must Produce

Goal: Analyze monthly sales over time using Moving Average and Linear Regression, then compare both methods and discuss limitations.

Task 1

Load the dataset, summarize it, plot sales over time, and describe trend, seasonality, irregular variation, and volatility.

Task 2

Calculate a 12-month moving average, plot it against sales, and explain what the smoothed line reveals.

Task 3

Fit a linear regression trend model, report the equation, R², slope, and business meaning.

Task 4

Compare the moving average and regression line using a plot, a table, and a written explanation.

Task 5

Write a limitations discussion explaining when each method helps and where each method can mislead.

Teaching point: the notebook already contains most of the technical pieces, but students must organize the outputs into the assignment deliverables.
NOTEBOOK ALIGNMENT

Are These Codes in the Jupyter Notebook?

Assignment TaskCovered in Notebook?What Students Still Need to Do
Task 1: Load + PlotYesAdd dataset summary, caption, and written pattern description.
Task 2: 12-Month MAYesShow first 15 rows with MA_12, explain why first 11 values are blank.
Task 3: RegressionYesReport equation, slope, R², and plain-English business meaning.
Task 4: Compare MethodsPartlyCreate a comparison table and justify which method gives the clearer trend view.
Task 5: LimitationsConceptualWrite the required discussion using evidence from the analysis.
Instructor guidance: Tell students not to submit code alone. They must submit screenshots, plots, captions, and written interpretation.
TASK 1

Task 1A: Load the Dataset and Inspect It

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Load the CSV file into a dataframe named df.
df = pd.read_csv('sales_data.csv')

# Convert Date from text into a true date column.
df['Date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['Date'])

# Sort rows so the time series is in chronological order.
df = df.sort_values('Date')

# Display the first 10 records for the required screenshot.
print(df.head(10))

# Print assignment summary facts.
print('Observations:', len(df))
print('Start date:', df['Date'].min())
print('End date:', df['Date'].max())
print(df['Sales'].describe())

What each line is doing

  • read_csv imports the sales file.
  • to_datetime makes the Date column usable for time-based plots and calculations.
  • sort_values prevents the line plot from connecting dates out of order.
  • describe gives mean, standard deviation, minimum, maximum, and quartiles.
Deliverable: screenshot of df.head(10), dataset period, number of observations, and basic Sales statistics.
TASK 1

Task 1B: Plot Sales Over Time

# Create a chart area with a readable size.
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5))

# Plot monthly sales against date.
plt.plot(df['Date'], df['Sales'], marker='o', linestyle='-')

# Add title and axis labels so the chart is self-explanatory.
plt.title('Monthly Sales Over Time')
plt.xlabel('Date')
plt.ylabel('Sales')

# Grid lines make trend and fluctuations easier to see.
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.show()
Caption template: “The time series plot shows a/an [upward/downward/stable] pattern, with [strong/weak] month-to-month fluctuation.”

This plot is the first evidence students use to describe trend, seasonality, irregular changes, and volatility.

TASK 1

Task 1C: Quantify the Patterns You Observed

# Month-over-month percent change shows volatility.
df['MoM_Change'] = df['Sales'].pct_change() * 100

# Absolute monthly change shows dollar movement.
df['MoM_Absolute'] = df['Sales'].diff()

# Extract month and year for seasonal analysis.
df['Month'] = df['Date'].dt.month
df['Year'] = df['Date'].dt.year

# Average sales by month helps reveal seasonality.
monthly_avg = df.groupby('Month')['Sales'].mean()

# Compare first half vs second half to describe trend.
first_half = df.iloc[:len(df)//2]['Sales'].mean()
second_half = df.iloc[len(df)//2:]['Sales'].mean()

# Percent change from first half to second half.
trend_pct = ((second_half - first_half) / first_half) * 100
print(trend_pct)

How to write the paragraph

  • Trend: use first-half vs. second-half average.
  • Seasonality: use average sales by month.
  • Volatility: use month-over-month changes.
  • Irregular variation: identify unusual spikes or drops.
Deliverable: 4–6 sentences. Do not just say “there is a trend.” State the direction and support it with numbers.
TASK 2

Task 2A: Calculate the 12-Month Moving Average

# A 12-month moving average uses the current month
# and the previous 11 months.
df['MA_12'] = df['Sales'].rolling(window=12).mean()

# Show the first 15 rows for the required screenshot.
print(df[['Date', 'Sales', 'MA_12']].head(15))

# Find the first row where MA_12 is available.
first_ma_row = df[df['MA_12'].notna()].iloc[0]

print('First MA date:', first_ma_row['Date'])
print('First MA value:', first_ma_row['MA_12'])

Why the first 11 values are NaN

A 12-month moving average cannot be calculated until Python has 12 sales values. January through November of the first year only have 1–11 months available, so those rows are blank. The first moving average appears in month 12.

MA12 = (Month 1 + Month 2 + ... + Month 12) / 12

Deliverable: screenshot showing Date, Sales, and MA_12 for the first 15–20 rows.

TASK 2

Task 2B: Plot Original Sales and Moving Average

The raw series is noisy. The 12-month moving average smooths the monthly ups and downs so students can see the underlying direction.

# Plot original sales as the noisy line.
plt.plot(df['Date'], df['Sales'], label='Original Sales')

# Plot moving average as the smoother trend line.
plt.plot(df['Date'], df['MA_12'], label='12-Month Moving Average', linewidth=3)

# Add labels so the submitted chart is professional.
plt.title('Original Sales vs. 12-Month Moving Average')
plt.xlabel('Date')
plt.ylabel('Sales')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.show()
Caption template: “The moving average removes seasonal noise and reveals a [clear upward/downward/stable] trend.”
TASK 2

Task 2C: Turn the Moving Average into Evidence

# Keep only rows where the moving average exists.
ma_data = df[df['MA_12'].notna()].copy()

# First and last moving-average values.
ma_start = ma_data.iloc[0]['MA_12']
ma_end = ma_data.iloc[-1]['MA_12']

# Dollar change in the smoothed trend.
ma_change = ma_end - ma_start

# Percent change makes the trend easier to interpret.
ma_pct_change = (ma_change / ma_start) * 100

print('MA start:', ma_start)
print('MA end:', ma_end)
print('MA change:', ma_change)
print('MA percent change:', ma_pct_change)

What students should write

  • The moving average starts at approximately $___ and ends at approximately $___.
  • This equals a change of $___, or ___%.
  • The smoothed line suggests the long-term sales trend is ___.
  • Because seasonal ups and downs are reduced, the manager can focus on the underlying growth direction.
TASK 3

Task 3A: Build the Linear Regression Trend Model

from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import r2_score

# Time_Index converts dates into numbers: 0, 1, 2, ...
df['Time_Index'] = range(len(df))

# X is the input: time.
X = df[['Time_Index']].values

# y is the outcome we are trying to explain: sales.
y = df['Sales'].values

# Fit the straight-line trend model.
reg_model = LinearRegression()
reg_model.fit(X, y)

# Intercept = predicted sales when Time_Index is 0.
intercept = reg_model.intercept_

# Slope = average sales change per month.
slope = reg_model.coef_[0]

# Regression_Pred gives the fitted trend line.
df['Regression_Pred'] = reg_model.predict(X)

# R² tells how much sales variation the line explains.
r_squared = r2_score(y, df['Regression_Pred'])

Terminology

  • Independent variable X: the predictor; here, time.
  • Dependent variable y: the result being explained; here, sales.
  • Intercept: starting point of the line.
  • Slope: average monthly increase or decrease.
  • R²: percent of sales variation explained by the line.
TASK 3

Task 3B: Plot and Interpret the Regression Line

The regression line summarizes the long-term average direction with one straight line.

# Print the equation students must report.
print(f'Sales = {intercept:,.2f} + {slope:,.2f} × Time')

# Convert monthly slope into annual growth.
annual_growth = slope * 12

print(f'Monthly increase: ${slope:,.2f}')
print(f'Annual increase: ${annual_growth:,.2f}')
print(f'R²: {r_squared:.4f}')

# Plot raw sales and the regression trend.
plt.plot(df['Date'], df['Sales'], label='Original Sales')
plt.plot(df['Date'], df['Regression_Pred'], label='Regression Line')
plt.legend()
plt.show()
Writing template: “The slope means sales increase by approximately $___ per month on average, or $___ per year.”
TASK 4

Task 4A: Compare Moving Average and Regression Visually

# Plot all three lines together.
plt.plot(df['Date'], df['Sales'], label='Original Sales')
plt.plot(df['Date'], df['MA_12'], label='12-Month Moving Average')
plt.plot(df['Date'], df['Regression_Pred'], label='Regression Trend')

# Add labels and legend for the required figure.
plt.title('Moving Average vs. Regression Trend')
plt.xlabel('Date')
plt.ylabel('Sales')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.show()
Key comparison: Moving average bends with the data. Regression forces one straight-line summary.

Students should use this visual to explain whether the smoothed trend and the straight-line trend tell the same story.

TASK 4

Task 4B: Create the Required Comparison Table

# Build a small table comparing both trend methods.
comparison_table = pd.DataFrame({
    'Method': ['12-Month Moving Average', 'Linear Regression'],

    # What each method produces.
    'Output': ['Smooth trend line', 'Straight trend line + equation'],

    # What each method is best for.
    'Best Use': ['Visual smoothing', 'Quantifying average growth'],

    # Main limitation students should discuss.
    'Main Limitation': ['Lags behind changes', 'Assumes one straight line']
})

print(comparison_table)

How to answer “which is clearer?”

If the question is about seeing the shape of the trend, the moving average is usually clearer because it smooths noise without forcing a straight line.

If the question is about reporting a single growth rate, regression is clearer because the slope gives a numeric monthly increase.

Best answer: explain that the methods answer different questions, then justify your choice using the plot.
TASK 5

Task 5: How to Write the Limitations Discussion

Moving Average Limitations

  • Lagging indicator: reacts late when sales change quickly.
  • Window-size sensitivity: 3, 6, and 12 months can tell different stories.
  • End-point problem: first 11 values are blank for a 12-month MA.
  • No equation: it does not give a formal slope or R².

Regression Limitations

  • Linearity assumption: assumes the trend is one straight line.
  • Ignores seasonality: monthly spikes and dips are treated as error.
  • Outlier sensitivity: unusual months can pull the slope up or down.
  • Time dependence: consecutive months are usually related, so independence is questionable.
Required writing standard: Do not simply list limitations. Explain why each limitation matters for a manager making decisions.
Strong sentence structure: “This matters because ______, so a manager could ______ if they relied on this method alone.”
SUBMISSION CHECKLIST

What Students Should Submit for Week 10

TaskSubmitQuality Check
1Data screenshot, time series plot, pattern paragraphIncludes trend, seasonality, irregular variation, and volatility.
2MA_12 screenshot, MA plot, 5–7 sentence interpretationExplains first 11 NaN values and what smoothing reveals.
3Regression equation, R², plot, slope interpretationExplains monthly and annual growth in plain language.
4Comparison plot, comparison table, 8–10 sentence answerJustifies which method is clearer and why.
52–3 paragraph limitations discussionIncludes at least 3 MA limitations and 3 regression limitations with examples.
Final reminder: Every plot needs a title, axis labels, legend when needed, and a caption that explains what the viewer should learn.
FORECASTING

Exponential Smoothing: Weight Recent Data More

St = α × Yt + (1 – α) × St-1
  • α (alpha) = smoothing parameter, 0 < α < 1
  • High α → reacts quickly to recent changes (volatile data)
  • Low α → stable, weights history more (smooth data)
  • More realistic than MA: recent data matters more than old data

Three Versions

📌 SES — Simple: no trend, no seasonality

📌 Holt — Double: captures trend only

📌 Holt-Winters — Triple: trend + seasonality (use this for our data!)

🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

The moving average treats all twelve months in its window equally. But think about that — should sales from two years ago have the same influence on next month's forecast as last month's actual sales? Probably not.

Exponential smoothing assigns weights that decay exponentially as observations get older. The most recent observation gets the highest weight, and the influence fades as you go back in time. For your sales data, which has both trend and seasonality, you will use the Holt-Winters model — also called Triple Exponential Smoothing. It is purpose-built for data like ours.

FORECASTING

The Three Things Holt-Winters Learns

L

Level

The current baseline value of the series. Think: “Where are sales now?”

T

Trend

The direction and speed of movement. Think: “Are sales growing or falling?”

S

Seasonality

The repeating calendar pattern. Think: “Which months are normally high or low?”

Forecast = Level + Trend + Seasonal pattern
That is why Holt-Winters is appropriate for our sales data: the notebook shows both upward trend and repeating seasonal movement.
TRAIN / TEST

Why We Split the Data Before Evaluating

Training data

The data the model is allowed to learn from. In the notebook, this is the first 160 rows.

Test data

The data we hide from the model until evaluation. In the notebook, this is the last 40 rows.

Purpose: We evaluate the model on unseen data because a model that only explains the past may fail in the future.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Students should understand that test data is like an exam. The model should not see the answers during training.

FORECASTING

Holt-Winters in Python

# Comment: Fit Holt-Winters on training data, then forecast the held-out test months.
from statsmodels.tsa.holtwinters import ExponentialSmoothing

# Split: reserve last 12 months for testing
train = df.iloc[:-12]
test  = df.iloc[-12:]

model = ExponentialSmoothing(
    train['Sales'],
    trend='add',
    seasonal='add',
    seasonal_periods=12
).fit()

forecast = model.forecast(12)

plt.plot(train['Sales'], label='Training')
plt.plot(test['Sales'],  label='Actual')
plt.plot(forecast,       label='Forecast')
plt.legend(); plt.show()
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Two things I want you to notice. First, we split the data into a training set and a test set. We train on everything except the last twelve months, then we forecast those twelve months and compare to what actually happened. This is how you evaluate a forecasting model — you test it on data it has never seen.

Second, notice seasonal_periods=12. This tells the model that one complete seasonal cycle is twelve months. The model then learns the seasonal pattern and the trend simultaneously, and combines them to project forward. This one function does a lot of heavy lifting.

FORECASTING

Holt-Winters Forecast Plot

What the blue line shows

The blue line is the historical sales series. It contains the pattern the model learns from: trend + repeating seasonality.

What the orange line shows

The orange line is the next 12-month forecast produced by Holt-Winters after fitting an additive trend and additive seasonal pattern.

How to interpret it

The forecast continues the upward trend while preserving the monthly seasonal movement. That is exactly why Holt-Winters is useful when data has both trend and seasonality.

Teaching point: Holt-Winters smooths the level, trend, and seasonality, then projects those components forward into future periods.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

This slide shows the actual notebook output for the future forecast. Students should see that Holt-Winters does not produce a flat line. Instead, it extends the long-run upward direction and preserves the recurring seasonal pattern. That makes it a very intuitive first forecasting method for business time series with monthly data.

If you want, you can also remind students that this is different from a simple moving average. A moving average only smooths the past. Holt-Winters uses smoothing to estimate components, and then it forecasts forward.

ARIMA

ARIMA: The Industry Standard

ARIMA = AutoRegressive + Integrated + Moving Average
AR(p)

AutoRegressive

Uses p past values as predictors. Last month's sales help predict this month's.

I(d)

Integrated

Differences the series d times to achieve stationarity. d=1 removes a linear trend.

MA(q)

Moving Average

Uses q past forecast errors as predictors. The model learns from its own mistakes.

ARIMA(p,d,q) — choose parameters using ACF/PACF plots, or use auto_arima to search automatically
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

ARIMA is the industry-standard forecasting model. ARIMA stands for AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average. The AR part uses past values of the series to predict the future — last month's sales help predict this month's. The I part applies differencing to make the series stationary. The MA part uses past forecast errors as predictors — it learns from its own mistakes.

These three components are controlled by three parameters: p for the AR order, d for how many times we difference, and q for the MA order. Choosing those parameters is the main skill — and we cover that on the next slide.

ARIMA TERMINOLOGY

ARIMA Terms in Everyday Language

TermPlain ExplanationExample
LagA previous value of the same series.Lag 1 = last month’s sales.
AutoregressionUsing past values to predict current or future values.Sales this month depends partly on sales last month.
DifferenceSubtracting one value from the prior value.March sales minus February sales.
Forecast errorThe gap between what the model predicted and what actually happened.Actual 300 minus forecast 290 = error of 10.
OrderThe number of past values, differences, or errors included.ARIMA(1,1,1) uses one AR lag, one difference, and one MA error term.
ARIMA TERMINOLOGY

ARIMA Terms in Plain English

AR(p)

Autoregression

Uses the series' own past values to predict the next value. If sales were high last month, that information helps predict this month.

Yt depends on Yt-1, Yt-2, ...
I(d)

Integrated / Differencing

Converts levels into changes so the series becomes more stable. This removes trend before ARIMA models the pattern.

Difference = Yt − Yt-1
MA(q)

Moving Average Error

Uses past forecast errors to improve the next forecast. This is different from the simple rolling average used for smoothing.

Forecast uses past errors εt-1, εt-2

Key terms students must know

TermPlain meaningWhy it matters
LagA past value of the same variable. Lag 1 means last period; lag 12 means same month last year.ARIMA learns from past values and past errors.
AutocorrelationThe relationship between today’s value and earlier values of the same series.If autocorrelation exists, ordinary regression assumptions are violated.
StationarityA series whose average and variation stay relatively stable over time.ARIMA works best after trend and seasonal drift are removed.
pHow many lagged values are included in the autoregressive part.Higher p means the model looks further back at previous observations.
dHow many times the data is differenced.d = 1 is common when the original series has an upward or downward trend.
qHow many lagged forecast errors are included in the moving-average error part.Helps correct predictable forecasting mistakes from recent periods.
SARIMASeasonal ARIMA: ARIMA plus seasonal terms (P,D,Q,m).Use it when monthly or quarterly data repeats in a seasonal pattern.
Important distinction: In ARIMA, moving average means using past forecast errors. It is not the same as the 12-month rolling average we used earlier to smooth the trend.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

This slide slows down the language behind ARIMA. Students often confuse the ARIMA moving-average term with a rolling average. Emphasize that ARIMA's MA term uses past forecast errors, while a rolling moving average uses past observed values to smooth the line.

The big idea is simple: ARIMA first stabilizes the series through differencing, then uses past values and past errors to forecast the next value. SARIMA adds a seasonal layer when the pattern repeats every 12 months, every 4 quarters, or another regular cycle.

DIAGNOSTICS

What ACF and PACF Are Trying to Tell Us

ACF

Shows how strongly sales are related to their own past values at different lags.

PACF

Shows the direct relationship with each lag after removing the effect of shorter lags.

Why it matters: these plots help us choose ARIMA parameters instead of guessing blindly.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Do not overteach ACF/PACF mathematically. The practical point is that time series often has memory, and these plots show how far that memory extends.

DIAGNOSTICS

Reading ACF and PACF Plots

ACF — Autocorrelation Function

  • Correlation of series with its own lags
  • Includes direct AND indirect effects
  • Seasonal: spikes at lags 12, 24, 36...
  • Use to choose q in ARIMA

PACF — Partial Autocorrelation

  • Direct correlation after removing indirect effects
  • Cuts off sharply for AR processes
  • More targeted than the ACF
  • Use to choose p in ARIMA
# Comment: Plot lag correlations to help choose ARIMA p and q parameters.
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf, plot_pacf
plot_acf(df['Sales'], lags=36)
plot_pacf(df['Sales'], lags=36)
plt.show()
Notebook output: ACF and PACF
Students can use these plots to discuss autocorrelation and choose ARIMA parameters.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

ACF and PACF plots are diagnostic tools that help you choose the right parameters for your ARIMA model. The ACF plot shows the correlation between the series and its own past values. If spikes appear at lags twelve and twenty-four for monthly data, that confirms a seasonal pattern. Use ACF to choose q.

The PACF plot shows the direct relationship between the series and each lag, after removing the influence of all shorter lags. Significant spikes in the PACF at lags one and two, followed by a sharp cutoff, suggests an AR(2) model. Use PACF to choose p.

ARIMA

Choosing ARIMA Parameters (p, d, q)

p — AR Order

  • Check PACF plot
  • Count spikes before cutoff
  • PACF cuts at lag 2 → p=2

d — Differencing

  • Run ADF test first
  • Non-stationary → d=1
  • Rarely need d > 2

q — MA Order

  • Check ACF plot
  • Count spikes before cutoff
  • ACF cuts at lag 1 → q=1
💡 Quick start: Try ARIMA(1,1,1) as your baseline — one round of differencing, one AR lag, one MA correction. Reliable for most business time series.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Selecting p, d, and q is where many students get stuck, so let me give you a practical workflow. Start with d: run the Augmented Dickey-Fuller test. If the series is non-stationary, set d equal to one. Then look at your PACF plot to choose p — count how many significant spikes appear before the plot cuts off to near zero. Do the same with the ACF plot for q.

If you just want to get started, try ARIMA(1,1,1) as your baseline. It handles simple trends and is a reasonable starting point for most business time series. Tune from there after seeing your accuracy metrics.

ARIMA

SARIMA: Adding the Seasonal Layer

SARIMA(p,d,q)(P,D,Q,m)

Non-Seasonal (p,d,q)

  • p — AR order (lags of the series)
  • d — Differencing order
  • q — MA order (lags of forecast errors)

Seasonal (P,D,Q,m)

  • P — Seasonal AR order
  • D — Seasonal differencing (usually 1)
  • Q — Seasonal MA order
  • m — Period (12 for monthly data)
For monthly sales with seasonality → start with SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,0,12)
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

When your data has both trend and seasonality, we extend ARIMA to SARIMA. SARIMA has a second set of parameters in parentheses that handle the seasonal component separately. The capital P, D, Q work exactly like their lowercase counterparts, but they operate at the seasonal lag — instead of looking at the previous month, they look at the same month last year.

For monthly data, m equals twelve. Start with a reasonable baseline like SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,0,12) and tune from there using AIC scores.

SARIMA TERMINOLOGY

What “Seasonal Lag 12” Means

Ordinary lag

Lag 1 compares this month to last month. Lag 2 compares this month to two months ago.

Seasonal lag

For monthly data, lag 12 compares this January to last January, this February to last February, and so on.

Monthly data: m = 12 because one full seasonal cycle has 12 months.
Why SARIMA helps: ARIMA handles short-term memory. SARIMA adds seasonal memory, so it can learn that the same month last year may matter.
EVALUATION

How Do We Know If Our Forecast Is Good?

MAE

Mean Absolute Error

avg(|Actual − Forecast|)

✓ Easy to interpret (same units)

✗ Treats all errors equally

RMSE

Root Mean Square Error

√avg((Actual − Forecast)²)

✓ Penalizes large errors more

✗ Sensitive to outliers

MAPE

Mean Absolute % Error

avg(|Error| / |Actual|) × 100

✓ Scale-free, comparable

✗ Undefined if actual = 0

Lower is better for all three. Use MAPE when comparing models across products or scales.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Building a forecast is only half the job. The other half is knowing whether it is accurate enough to trust. MAE is the average absolute difference between our forecast and reality — easy to explain to a non-technical audience. RMSE squares the errors before averaging, which means large errors get penalized much more heavily. MAPE expresses accuracy as a percentage — great for comparing across different products or scales.

A MAPE below ten percent is generally considered excellent for business forecasting. But always interpret in context — for extremely volatile data, twenty percent might actually be quite good. When you report your model accuracy, always include the metric AND interpret what it means for the decisions being made.

EVALUATION

Calculating Forecast Accuracy in Python

# Comment: Compare actual test values to forecasted values using MAE, RMSE, and MAPE.
import numpy as np

actual        = test['Sales'].values
forecast_vals = model.forecast(12).values

MAE  = np.mean(np.abs(actual - forecast_vals))
RMSE = np.sqrt(np.mean((actual - forecast_vals)**2))
MAPE = np.mean(np.abs((actual - forecast_vals) / actual)) * 100

print(f"MAE:  {{MAE:.2f}}")
print(f"RMSE: {{RMSE:.2f}}")
print(f"MAPE: {{MAPE:.2f}}%")
MAPE <10% = Excellent 10–20% = Good 20–50% = Acceptable >50% = Poor — revisit model
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Here is how you calculate all three metrics with just a few lines of NumPy. The key is that you compare your forecast values against the actual test values — the data you held out before fitting.

Remember: these benchmarks are rules of thumb. When you report your model accuracy, always say what the number means in context. 'MAPE of 7.3%' alone is meaningless to most managers. 'Our forecast is off by less than 8% on average' is something they can act on.

METRICS

Forecast Accuracy Terms in Plain English

MAE

Average absolute miss. If MAE is 7, the forecast is off by about 7 sales units on average.

RMSE

Large-error penalty. RMSE becomes bigger when the model makes large mistakes.

MAPE

Average percent miss. If MAPE is 2.6%, the forecast is off by about 2.6% on average.

For non-technical audiences, MAPE is often easiest to explain because it is a percentage.
MANUAL CALCULATION

How MAE, RMSE, and MAPE Are Calculated by Hand

Forecast accuracy is calculated by comparing each actual test value to its forecasted value.
PeriodActualForecastError|Error|
Example 1300294+66
Example 2290296-66
Example 3310301+99

MAE

(6 + 6 + 9) / 3 = 7.00

RMSE

sqrt((6² + 6² + 9²)/3)
= sqrt(51) = 7.14

MAPE

avg(6/300, 6/290, 9/310) × 100
≈ 2.33%
Notebook best model: SARIMA produced MAPE = 2.57%, slightly better than Holt-Winters at 2.62%.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Use this slide to demystify the formulas. Students should understand that all three metrics start from the same basic error column: actual minus forecast.

WORKFLOW

The Complete Forecasting Workflow

  1. 1
    Load & Explore — Import data, set date index, plot raw series
  2. 2
    Identify Components — Spot trend, seasonality, and outliers visually
  3. 3
    Test Stationarity — Run ADF test; apply differencing if needed (d=1)
  4. 4
    Decompose — Run seasonal_decompose to isolate Trend, Seasonal, Residual
  5. 5
    Fit Models — Try Moving Average, Holt-Winters, ARIMA; compare AIC
  6. 6
    Evaluate — Compute MAE, RMSE, MAPE on held-out test set only
  7. 7
    Forecast & Report — Generate future values; present with confidence intervals
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Let me consolidate everything into a single, repeatable workflow you can use for any time series problem — not just this week's assignment, but in your careers. Step one: load and plot. Step two: identify components visually. Step three: test for stationarity. Step four: decompose. Step five: fit candidate models. Step six: evaluate on test data. Step seven: forecast and communicate with uncertainty.

Follow it every time. Do not skip steps. The steps that look like overhead — like testing for stationarity — are the ones that save you from presenting a model that violates its own assumptions.

MODEL SELECTION

Which Forecasting Method Should I Use?

ScenarioRecommended MethodWhy
Need to see the trend visuallyMoving Average (12-month)Smooths noise without assumptions
Need to report trend rate to managementLinear RegressionProvides slope with statistical test
Data has clear seasonality + trendHolt-Winters (Triple ES)Purpose-built for exactly this case
Complex seasonal + autocorrelated dataSARIMAMost powerful for time series
Need a quick baseline forecastARIMA(1,1,1)Reliable, low-parameter starting point
Start simple → evaluate → add complexity only if accuracy does not improve enough.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Here is your decision table. Use it every time you approach a new forecasting problem. Start with the simplest method that fits the scenario. If you just need to show a trend to a stakeholder, a twelve-month moving average is perfect. If your data has both trend and seasonality — which is true for sales_data.csv — Holt-Winters is your first choice.

The key principle is parsimony: use the simplest model that achieves acceptable accuracy. Complex models are harder to explain, harder to maintain, and more prone to overfitting.

NOTEBOOK RESULTS

Actual Forecast Model Comparison from the Notebook

Notebook output: model comparison
ModelMAERMSEMAPEInterpretation
SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1,12)7.238.132.57%Best overall accuracy
Holt-Winters7.388.532.62%Very close second; easier to explain
ARIMA(1,1,1)21.8924.537.65%Weak because it misses seasonality
Teaching point: The best statistical model is not always the best communication model. SARIMA is slightly more accurate, while Holt-Winters may be easier for managers to understand.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Connect the accuracy metrics to a managerial conclusion. The class should see why a seasonal method beats a non-seasonal ARIMA model for this dataset.

MODEL WARNING

Why a Machine Learning Model Did Not Win Here

Notebook result

Random Forest with lag features had MAPE ≈ 9.95%, worse than SARIMA and Holt-Winters.

Why this can happen

Machine learning models need carefully engineered time features. A specialized time-series model can outperform a general-purpose model when trend and seasonality are strong.

More complex does not automatically mean more accurate.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

This is a useful lesson for managerial decision-making: use the model that performs best and is understandable, not the model with the most impressive name.

NOTEBOOK INTERPRETATION

How to Choose the Best Forecast Model

ModelMAERMSEMAPEPlain-English Meaning
SARIMA7.238.132.57%Best overall in the notebook; lowest errors.
Holt-Winters7.388.532.62%Nearly as good and easier to explain.
ARIMA21.8924.537.65%Worse because it does not model seasonality as directly.
Teaching conclusion: SARIMA wins numerically, but Holt-Winters is also strong and easier for beginners to understand.
INTERPRETATION

Forecast Uncertainty: Confidence Intervals

No forecast is perfectly certain. A confidence interval shows the range where the true future value is likely to fall.
  • 95% CI: We expect the actual value to fall within this band 95% of the time
  • CIs widen as we forecast further into the future — more uncertainty over time
  • Narrow CI = model fits well; Wide CI = high uncertainty, use with caution
  • Always show CIs in presentations — it is intellectually honest
  • A point forecast without uncertainty bands can mislead decision-makers
# Comment: Extract the forecast and its lower/upper confidence limits for plotting uncertainty.
forecast_obj = model.get_forecast(12)
summary = forecast_obj.summary_frame()
# Columns: mean, mean_ci_lower, mean_ci_upper
plt.plot(summary['mean'], label='Forecast')
plt.fill_between(summary.index,
    summary['mean_ci_lower'],
    summary['mean_ci_upper'],
    alpha=0.3, label='95% CI')
plt.legend()
Notebook output: future sales forecast
The notebook’s final Holt-Winters forecast extends the historical trend and seasonal pattern 12 months ahead.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

One of the biggest mistakes junior analysts make is presenting a single-line forecast without any indication of uncertainty. Every forecast is a probability distribution, not a single point. The confidence interval shows the range within which we expect the actual value to land, ninety-five times out of one hundred.

Notice that as you forecast further into the future, the bands widen. This is correct and expected. When you present your forecast to a leadership team, always include these bands. A forecast that claims false precision will undermine your credibility the moment reality deviates from the single line you drew.

COMMUNICATION

Communicating Forecasts to Non-Technical Audiences

✗ 'MAPE = 7.3%, RMSE = 4.12'
✓ 'Our model is accurate to within 7% — our forecast of 500 units could be off by roughly 35 units.'
✗ Show ACF/PACF plots in a board meeting
✓ Show only the forecast chart with a confidence band. Put diagnostics in a technical appendix.
✗ 'Sales will be exactly $1.4M in Q4.'
✓ 'We forecast $1.3–1.5M in Q4 with 95% confidence, assuming no major disruptions.'
The best forecast is useless if the decision-maker does not trust or understand it.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

One of the most important skills in your careers will not be building the model — it will be translating the model into language that drives decisions. A city council member does not know what MAPE means. A hospital CFO does not need to see a PACF plot.

Translate your metrics into business language. Report uncertainty in plain terms. And always frame your forecast in the context of the decision it is meant to support. The model is the means. The decision is the end.

A good forecast should become a planning table. Students should be able to translate a line plot into quantities that support inventory, staffing, or budget decisions.

FORECAST OUTPUT

Next 12-Month Forecast from the Notebook

Future StepForecast
1294.60
