🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

This slide bridges analytics to action. The forecast values are not just numbers; they drive inventory, staffing, and budgeting decisions.

MBA TRACK

MBA Application: Sales & Inventory Forecasting

Case: Predict monthly product sales to optimize inventory and avoid costly stockouts.

❓ Business Question

  • How much inventory do we order for Q4?
  • When does demand peak — Nov or Dec?
  • Is our growth rate accelerating or slowing?

🔧 Approach

  • Holt-Winters for trend + seasonal
  • SARIMA for precise seasonal modeling
  • Report slope: $/month growth rate

✅ Managerial Action

  • Order 20% more stock 6 weeks before peak
  • Negotiate pre-season supplier contracts
  • Tie inventory investment to forecasted revenue
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Suppose you manage the retail division of a consumer goods company. You have monthly sales data going back five years. Your purchasing team needs to know how much inventory to order for the fourth quarter — and they need that number eight weeks before the season starts, because of supplier lead times.

You apply Holt-Winters or SARIMA to your historical data, which captures the seasonal peak in November and December. The model tells you to expect a thirty-two percent sales increase in November compared to the annual average. You use that number to negotiate pre-season inventory commitments with your supplier at a volume discount. That is the direct business value of a good forecast.

MHA TRACK

MHA Application: Patient Volume Forecasting

Case: Forecast monthly ER admissions for proactive nurse staffing and resource planning.

Seasonal ER Pattern (illustrative)

Jan (peak)Jun (low)Dec (peak)

Staffing Implications

  • Forecast Jan–Feb surge 90 days out → recruit travel nurses in October
  • Reduce per diem staff in June–August (low season)
  • Tie on-call scheduling to seasonal index
  • Budget for winter supply increases in advance
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

For the healthcare administrators — here is your scenario. ER admissions follow a strong seasonal pattern: they peak in winter due to flu and respiratory illness, and they drop in summer. If you can forecast that January will see thirty-two percent more admissions than August, you can alert your HR team in October to begin recruiting travel nurses for January.

The cost of over-staffing by ten percent is far lower than the cost of a dangerous nurse-to-patient ratio during a flu surge. This is where forecasting directly impacts patient safety — not just the budget.

MPA TRACK

MPA Application: Revenue & Utility Demand Forecasting

Case: Forecast annual tax revenue and utility demand for infrastructure budget planning.

Revenue Trend (illustrative, $M)

201920222026F

■ Forecasted   ■ Actual

Policy Uses

  • Set realistic annual budget ceilings from revenue forecast
  • Time bond issuance when cash flow dips are predicted
  • Plan infrastructure before demand outstrips capacity
  • Justify staffing requests to elected officials with data
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

For the public administrators — your application is equally high-stakes. Municipal governments are required by law to pass balanced budgets. Overestimate revenue and you face mid-year cuts to essential services. Underestimate and you leave money on the table.

A time series forecast of tax revenue gives your CFO a defensible, data-driven basis for the budget proposal. You are not just guessing. You have a model. Similarly, forecasting utility demand means your water treatment plant and electrical grid are scaled for the population five years from now, not for the population from five years ago.

ACTIVITY 1

Class Activity 1: Visualizing Time Series

Dataset: sales_data.csv  |  Tool: Google Colab — SalesTimeSeries.ipynb
  1. 1
    Load sales_data.csv and set Date as the index using parse_dates
  2. 2
    Plot the time series of Sales over time using matplotlib
  3. 3
    Add a 12-month rolling mean to the same plot to show the smoothed trend
  4. 4
    Add axis labels, a chart title, and a legend to your chart
  5. 5
    Write 2–3 sentences describing the patterns you observe
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Activity 1 is your hands-on warm-up for this week. You are going to open the SalesTimeSeries.ipynb notebook in Google Colab, load the sales_data.csv file, and do three things: plot the raw series, overlay the twelve-month rolling mean, and write a brief description of what you observe.

Pay close attention to how you label your axes and your legend. A chart without labels is not a professional deliverable. Take about fifteen minutes on this, then we will discuss what everyone found as a class.

ACTIVITY 2

Activity 2: Trend Analysis — Question Guide

Q1 — Plot & Describe
Describe trend direction, seasonal patterns, and any unusual spikes or dips in 2–3 sentences.
Q2 — 12-Month Moving Average
df['MA12'] = df['Sales'].rolling(12).mean() — overlay on same plot. Describe what the smoothed line reveals vs. the raw series.
Q3 — Linear Regression Trend
Create time_idx, fit LinearRegression, plot the trend line, and report the slope in plain language (e.g., "sales grow by X units/month on average").
Q4 — Compare MA vs. Regression
MA shows the shape of the trend; Regression quantifies the rate of change. They are complementary — use both for different purposes.
Q5 — Discuss Limitations
MA loses data and lags; Regression assumes linearity and independent observations — a key assumption violated by time series data.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Let me walk you through Activity 2 before you start. Question one asks you to plot and describe — do not just make the chart, write two or three sentences about what you see. Question two: the one line of code is straightforward, but the description is where the grade is.

Question three: report the slope numerically and interpret it. Question four: this is an analytical comparison question — the key insight is that they are complementary, not competing. Question five: show me you understand the assumptions being violated. That is what separates good analysts from great ones.

TIPS

Common Mistakes to Avoid

✗ Forgetting parse_dates when loading CSV
✓ Always use parse_dates=['Date'], index_col='Date' — verify with df.index.dtype
✗ Fitting ARIMA on non-stationary data
✓ Run ADF test first; difference until stationary (p < 0.05), then fit ARIMA
✗ Evaluating accuracy on training data
✓ Always hold out a test set before fitting; evaluate only on the held-out test set
✗ Reporting 'MAPE = 8.3%' with no interpretation
✓ Say: 'MAPE of 8.3% = excellent accuracy — our forecast is off by less than 9% on average'
✗ Choosing the model with the best training fit
✓ Compare models on test-set performance only; lower out-of-sample error wins
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Let me share the five most common mistakes I see in student submissions. First: forgetting to parse dates — your plot will show numbers instead of dates, and nothing will work correctly. Second: fitting ARIMA before testing for stationarity — this invalidates your model entirely. Third: evaluating your model on the training data — that is like grading yourself on an open-book exam you wrote yourself.

Fourth: reporting MAPE as a bare number without saying what it means. Fifth: choosing your model based on training fit instead of test set performance. The goal is generalization, not memorization. A model that fits training data perfectly but fails on new data is worse than useless.

CODE REFERENCE

Week 10 Complete Code Reference

# 1. IMPORTS
import pandas as pd, numpy as np, matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from statsmodels.tsa.seasonal import seasonal_decompose
from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import adfuller
from statsmodels.tsa.holtwinters import ExponentialSmoothing
from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA

# 2. LOAD
df = pd.read_csv('sales_data.csv', parse_dates=['Date'], index_col='Date')

# 3. VISUALIZE
df['Sales'].plot(title='Monthly Sales'); plt.show()

# 4. MOVING AVERAGE
df['MA12'] = df['Sales'].rolling(12).mean()
df[['Sales','MA12']].plot(); plt.show()

# 5. ADF TEST
result = adfuller(df['Sales']); print(f"p-value: {{result[1]:.4f}}")

# 6. DECOMPOSE
seasonal_decompose(df['Sales'], model='additive', period=12).plot(); plt.show()

# 7. HOLT-WINTERS
train, test = df.iloc[:-12], df.iloc[-12:]
hw = ExponentialSmoothing(train['Sales'], trend='add', seasonal='add', seasonal_periods=12).fit()
hw.forecast(12).plot(); plt.show()

# 8. ARIMA(1,1,1)
arima = ARIMA(train['Sales'], order=(1,1,1)).fit()
arima.forecast(12)
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Here is your all-in-one code reference for Week 10. The entire pipeline from loading data to generating ARIMA forecasts is contained in these few lines. Notice the logical progression: load, visualize, smooth, test stationarity, decompose, fit models. This is exactly the seven-step workflow we covered on slide 26.

Keep this slide handy while working on your notebook. In your actual notebook, each of these blocks will have more detail, chart labels, and written interpretations — but this skeleton is the minimum viable pipeline.

CODE REFERENCE

Commented Beginner Version of the Core Workflow

# Step 1: Import tools for data, math, charts, and forecasting.
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from statsmodels.tsa.holtwinters import ExponentialSmoothing

# Step 2: Load the sales data and turn Date into a real date column.
df = pd.read_csv('sales_data.csv')
df['Date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['Date'])
df = df.sort_values('Date').set_index('Date')

# Step 3: Plot the historical series before modeling.
df['Sales'].plot(title='Monthly Sales Over Time')
plt.show()

# Step 4: Smooth the series with a 12-month moving average.
df['MA_12'] = df['Sales'].rolling(window=12).mean()

# Step 5: Train Holt-Winters because the data has trend and seasonality.
model = ExponentialSmoothing(df['Sales'], trend='add', seasonal='add', seasonal_periods=12).fit()

# Step 6: Forecast the next 12 future months.
forecast = model.forecast(12)
Every line either prepares the data, reveals a pattern, trains a model, or produces a forecast.
SUMMARY

Week 10 Key Takeaways

  1. 1
    Time series = measurements at equal intervals; always visualize before modeling.
  2. 2
    Four components: Trend (direction), Seasonality (cycles), Cyclical (long waves), Irregular (noise).
  3. 3
    Stationarity is required for ARIMA — test with ADF; fix with differencing (d=1 removes linear trend).
  4. 4
    Moving Average shows the trend's shape; Linear Regression quantifies the rate — use both.
  5. 5
    Holt-Winters handles trend + seasonality; ARIMA/SARIMA is the industry-standard for complex data.
  6. 6
    Evaluate on held-out test data using MAE, RMSE, and MAPE — never on training data alone.
  7. 7
    Present forecasts with confidence bands and interpret in plain business language for decision-makers.
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Let me bring it all together. Seven key takeaways for Week 10. One: always plot first. Two: every series has four components. Three: stationarity is a prerequisite for ARIMA. Four: moving average and regression serve different purposes — use both. Five: Holt-Winters and SARIMA are your power tools. Six: evaluate on test data only. Seven: translate results into plain language.

These seven points should guide every time series analysis you do from here forward — not just in this course, but in your careers.

STUDENT CHECKLIST

Before You Submit: Can You Explain These?

Concepts

  • What makes data a time series?
  • What are trend, seasonality, cycle, and noise?
  • What is stationarity and why does ARIMA require it?
  • What does differencing do?

Notebook outputs

  • What does the moving average show?
  • What does the regression slope mean?
  • Which model had the lowest error?
  • How should a manager use the 12-month forecast?
If you can explain these in your own words, you understand the lecture.

🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Before we wrap up, here are your deliverables. Activity 1 should be completed and submitted before you leave class today — just the visualization with the rolling mean and your written description. Activity 2 is your take-home assignment — all five questions on trend analysis, due by the next class session.

Use the SalesTimeSeries.ipynb notebook and the sales_data.csv file. Everything is posted on Blackboard. Next week we are returning to regression — but this time in a multivariate context. Great work today.

BONUS

Bonus: Auto-Select Parameters with pmdarima

# Install if needed: pip install pmdarima
from pmdarima import auto_arima

model = auto_arima(
    train['Sales'],
    seasonal=True,
    m=12,
    stepwise=True,
    information_criterion='aic',
    trace=True
)
print(model.summary())
forecast = model.predict(12)
  • auto_arima tests many (p,d,q)(P,D,Q) combinations and selects the model with the lowest AIC
  • AIC — Akaike Information Criterion: measures fit while penalizing complexity. Lower = better.
  • trace=True shows the search process — great for learning how the selection works
  • Use as a time-saver; understand the manual workflow first so you can interpret the output
🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

Once you understand how to manually select p, d, and q, you can use auto_arima from the pmdarima library to automate the search. It tests many combinations of parameters and selects the one with the best AIC score — the Akaike Information Criterion, which balances model fit against complexity.

I recommend using auto_arima to check your manual selection: if they agree, you have strong confidence in your choice. If they disagree, investigate why — that discrepancy is usually educational. But do not use auto_arima as a substitute for understanding the manual workflow.

BONUS

Bonus: Residual Diagnostics

# Built-in diagnostic plots (4 panels)
arima_model.plot_diagnostics(figsize=(12,8))
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

# Ljung-Box test: are residuals white noise?
from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import acorr_ljungbox
lb = acorr_ljungbox(arima_model.resid, lags=[12], return_df=True)
print(lb)  # p > 0.05 → residuals are white noise ✓

1 — Residual Plot

Should look like random noise — no patterns or trends

2 — Histogram

Residuals should be roughly bell-shaped (normal distribution)

3 — Q-Q Plot

Points should fall along the diagonal reference line

4 — Ljung-Box p > 0.05

Confirms residuals are not autocorrelated ✓

🎙 PRESENTER NOTES▼

A final quality check before you trust your forecast: residual diagnostics. After fitting your ARIMA model, examine the residuals — the differences between what the model predicted and what actually happened. Ideally, residuals should look like white noise: random, no patterns, roughly normally distributed, and not correlated with each other.

The built-in plot_diagnostics method gives you four diagnostic plots at once. The Ljung-Box test formally tests whether any autocorrelation remains in the residuals. A p-value above 0.05 means the residuals are statistically indistinguishable from white noise — your model has captured all the structure it can. If patterns remain, your model is misspecified and needs adjustment.
